notes.claim: On September 18, 1931, the tracks of the South Manchuria Railway were blown up (Liyutao Lake Incident)
notes.context: A self-produced bombing incident planned by Kanji Ishihara and Seishiro Itagaki, staff officers of the Kwantung Army. This was used as an excuse for the incident as being the work of the Chinese military. The postwar Tokyo Trials and Japanese historical research also confirmed that this was a plot by the Kwantung Army.

sources.title: Far East International Military Tribunal Records (in Japanese)

notes.claim: The Lytton Commission of the League of Nations submits a report disapproving of Manchukuo
notes.context: In 1932, the League of Nations dispatched an investigation team headed by Lord Lytton. Although the report acknowledged Japan's special rights and interests in Manchuria, it concluded that the Manchuria Incident could not be recognized as Japan's legitimate defense, and that Manchukuo could not have been established without the support of the Japanese military. The report was adopted at the League of Nations general meeting in February 1933, and Japan withdrew from the League of Nations.

sources.title: Report of the Commission of Inquiry (Lytton Report)

notes.context: March 1, 1932 Declaration of the Founding of the Nation. The era name is "Daido". Puyi, the last emperor of the Qing Dynasty, was appointed as the regent (later emperor), but it was essentially a puppet state ruled by the Kwantung Army. Internationally, only a few countries, including Japan, Germany, and Italy, have approved it.
